reforms.description: The government aims to achieve a 5 percent share of R&D expenditure in total GNP by 2001, with an intermediate goal of raising it to 3.5 percent by 1996. This includes a significant increase in spending on science and technology in the 1994 budget, aimed at enhancing conditions for economic growth. Additionally, a five-year programme for financial liberalisation was introduced to overcome inefficiencies in credit allocation and transition to a more market-oriented financial system.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Increasing R&D expenditure and enhancing financial systems are expected to boost productivity and innovation.
reforms.status_evidence: The 1994 budget includes a significant increase in spending on science and technology.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reform represents a structural change in R&D funding and financial systems.
reforms.source_quote: Looking ahead, policies are aimed at achieving a 5 percent share of R&D expenditure in total GNP by 2001. The intermediate goal is to raise the share of expenditure to 3.5 percent of GDP by 1996.

reforms.description: A Science and Technology Promotion Fund will be created to make subsidised loans for funding core technologies and to subsidise basic technological projects.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Subsidised loans for core technologies are expected to enhance innovation and technological development.
reforms.status_evidence: The fund is planned but not yet implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: The establishment of a fund represents a significant but narrower reform in innovation support.
reforms.source_quote: A Science and Technology Promotion Fund will be created. This fund will be empowered to make subsidised loans for funding core technologies and to subsidise basic technological projects.

reforms.description: The government plans to increase the proportion of science and technology graduates from universities to 55 percent by 1996, aiming to enhance the skilled workforce for R&D. This includes providing subsidies to private sector colleges to increase the proportion of science and engineering graduates from 40% to 55%.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Increasing the number of skilled graduates is expected to enhance the capacity for R&D and innovation.
reforms.status_evidence: The plan to increase graduate proportions is set for 1996.
reforms.importance_rationale: The initiative represents a significant effort to improve the skilled workforce for R&D.
reforms.source_quote: At the university level, subsidies will be provided to the private sector colleges (which account for 75 per cent of total enrolment) as a means to raise the proportion of science and engineering graduates to 55 per cent of the total from 40 per cent in 1992.
